nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q1.alt: Question 1. According to the narrator, what makes playing basketball appealing? Choices: A: She likes the feeling of knowing exactly what to do when she plays basketball. B: She feels the skills needed for playing basketball are simple to master. C: She thinks that when she plays basketball she is likely to succeed. D: She gets to be like her brothers when she plays basketball.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q2.alt: Question 2. According to the narrator, why is basketball “as good as Shakespeare” (lines 16 and 17)? Choices: A: It connects her to important people. B: It helps her to understand her life. C: It has the power to strongly affect her. D: It helps her deal with disappointments in life.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q3.alt: Question 3. Based on lines 20 through 29, what can readers conclude about the narrator's father? Choices: A: He thinks boys and girls should pursue different activities. B: He believes that siblings should support one another. C: He values sports more than he values education. D: He wishes that all of his children were boys.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q4.alt: Question 4. What does the narrator discover in lines 30 through 36 that affects the plot of the story? Choices: A: Watching basketball gives her the same feeling as studying. B: She wants other people to pay more attention to her. C: She is jealous of the opportunity her brother has. D: Her passion for basketball is overwhelming.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q5.alt: Question 5. How does the incident described in lines 53 through 59 affect the story? Choices: A: It shows how the narrator and her family attempt to compromise. B: It escalates the conflict so that the father is forced to make a choice. C: It reveals the influence the mother and brother have on the father’s behavior. D: It demonstrates the father’s beliefs and his realization that his beliefs are wrong.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q6.alt: Question 6. How does the structure of the story contribute to its meaning? Choices: A: The narrator describes how she differs from her brothers, which leads to a conflict with her father. B: The narrator expresses her point of view, which provides background for the conflict with her father. C: The narrator describes her frustration with life at home and school, which leads to problems with her father. D: The narrator begins with a reflection on rules, which leads to an improvement in her relationship with her father.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q7.alt: Question 7. How does the basketball game setting relate to a theme of the story? Choices: A: by showing where the narrator feels the most alive B: by showing how the narrator utilizes her knowledge C: by showing how the narrator differs from other girls D: by showing why the narrator supports her brother

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q22.alt: Question 22. Read these sentences from line 3. My friends were fond of practical jokes. I do not care to know people who are not. These statements impact the story by explaining Choices: A: why the narrator laughs so hard at dinner B: the narrator’s presence at the dinner C: the narrator’s reason for suspicion D: why the guests embraced him

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q23.alt: Question 23. What does the phrase “as a dog scents game” in line 14 of the story suggest about the narrator’s actions? Choices: A: He is enjoying the laughter of the other guests. B: He feels on the verge of finding the prankster. C: He feels left out of the dinner conversation. D: He is alert to some hidden plan.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q24.alt: Question 24. Read lines 14 and 15 of the story. I did not let a word, or a meaning, or a gesture escape me. This sentence suggests that the narrator Choices: A: feels anxious about his current situation B: is highly critical of his friends’ interactions C: feels defenseless against the coming events D: is too distracted to join his friends’ conversation

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q25.alt: Question 25. How do lines 25 through 33 build suspense? Choices: A: They suggest that someone is looking in the window. B: They extend the narrator’s search for deceit. C: They indicate unusual details about the room. D: They reveal the narrator’s fear of the dark.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q26.alt: Question 26. Which words reveal the irony of the narrator’s situation? Choices: A: “I thought: ‘Here are people who have more than their share of amusement, and apparently without reason. They must have planned some good joke.’” (lines 9 through 11) B: “The hour struck for retiring; and the whole household came to escort me to my room.” (line 17) C: “However, the night was advancing; and I ended by coming to the conclusion that I was foolish.” (lines 35 and 36) D: “The precautions I had taken in closing the shutters and going to sleep in the middle of the room had only brought about the practical joke I had been trying to avoid.” (lines 68 and 69)

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q27.alt: Question 27. What effect does the point of view of the story have on the reader? Choices: A: The point of view increases suspense by limiting the reader to the narrator’s understanding of events. B: The point of view creates humor by showing how the narrator's friends misunderstand him. C: The point of view raises the reader’s sympathy for the questions the narrator faces. D: The point of view adds to the reader’s mistrust of what the narrator reports.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q28.alt: Question 28. Which detail best expresses a theme of the story? Choices: A: “One autumn I went to spend the hunting season with some friends . . .” (line 1) B: “Assuredly I am to be the victim . . .” (line 11) C: “I heard persons moving about outside my door.” (line 23) D: “For at least another hour I remained awake, starting at the slightest sound.” (line 51)

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q29.alt: Question 29. Which words from the article provide the best clue to the meaning of the word “tedious” in line 3? Choices: A: “Confession Time” (line 1) B: “head flat on desk” (lines 1 and 2) C: “takes an eternity to tick by” (line 4) D: “You begin to wish you had your cell phone” (lines 4 and 5)

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q30.alt: Question 30. Read this sentence from line 6 of the article. Boredom is the enemy of fun. Which quotation best helps the reader understand this sentence? Choices: A: “If fun is the big drop of a roller coaster, boredom is waiting in line.” (line 7) B: “If we want to figure out the future of fun, we have to first figure out how to end boredom.” (line 8) C: “Imagine that: a world where each second is interesting and nothing is dull.” (line 9) D: “That sounds like science fiction, but new psychological research hints that being bored—even in class—may one day be a thing of the past.” (lines 9 through 11)

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q31.alt: Question 31. Lines 19 through 26 connect modern entertainment to boredom by suggesting that it causes us to Choices: A: want more challenging games to play B: misunderstand the role of fun in our lives C: develop expectations for high levels of excitement D: avoid adapting to new technological innovations

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q32.alt: Question 32. How does Professor Eastwood’s work differ from previous research? Choices: A: by attempting to create a new definition for boredom B: by failing to look into a connection to brain activity C: by focusing on the human attention system D: by using data from earlier studies on boredom

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q33.alt: Question 33. Read lines 9 through 11 of the article. That sounds like science fiction, but new psychological research hints that being bored—even in class—may one day be a thing of the past. Which quotation provides the best evidence to support this claim? Choices: A: “. . . less than one in 20 adults were bored at work.” (lines 15 and 16) B: “. . . our brains adapt really quickly to certain levels of stimulation . . .” (lines 23 and 24) C: “. . . anything the attention system in your brain locks onto will be automatically sucked up into your conscious awareness . . .” (lines 43 and 44) D: “Each of us has the ability to make every situation interesting; all we need to do is change the way we look at it . . .” (lines 61 and 62)

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q34.alt: Question 34. The final sentence in lines 68 and 69 best contributes to the article by Choices: A: suggesting that the author often fell asleep in classes B: connecting the information to the incident described at the beginning C: encouraging students to stay awake and avoid boredom in class D: emphasizing that teenagers are often more bored than other groups

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q35.alt: Question 35. Which sentence states a central idea that is supported throughout the article? Choices: A: Modern entertainment decreases boredom. B: Boredom is present in teenagers more than in adults. C: Everyone experiences boredom occasionally. D: People have the ability to control their boredom.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q36.alt: Question 36. How was CATIA essential to the success of Gehry’s plan for the museum? Choices: A: It helped him find less expensive materials. B: It allowed him to work earlier ideas into his design. C: It suggested unique materials to use for construction. D: It allowed him to add less-common shapes into his design.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q37.alt: Question 37. What do lines 25 through 30 contribute to the development of ideas in the article? Choices: A: They explain a way of thinking that allowed an obstacle to be overcome. B: They show how preferences in the shapes of buildings have changed. C: They describe a step in the building process. D: They illustrate how different artists work.

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q38.alt: Question 38. The phrase “waved a magic wand” (line 40) suggests that the Choices: A: museum fulfilled the city’s wishes B: project’s success amazed many people C: project succeeded in part because of luck D: museum resembled a creation from a fairy tale

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q39.alt: Question 39. According to the article, what quality is the author referring to by comparing the Bilbao museum to the Eiffel Tower and the Pyramids (lines 67 through 69)? Choices: A: the impact of its height B: the ability to generate revenue C: the effort needed to construct it D: the widespread amazement felt about it

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q40.alt: Question 40. Which phrase has the strongest connection to the photo? Choices: A: “. . . someone must have waved a magic wand.” (line 40) B: “. . . blossoming like silver petals . . .” (line 48) C: “. . . connecting the urban fabric of a fairly dense city . . .” (lines 52 and 53) D: “I’m trying to get more liquid . . .” (line 83)

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q41.alt: Question 41. Which sentence best supports the central idea of the article? Choices: A: “Three architects from Austria, Japan, and the United States were invited to submit proposals.” (lines 12 and 13) B: “Gehry already had used CATIA for parts of several other buildings, including Fred and Ginger in Prague.” (lines 22 and 23) C: “Instead more than a million came to marvel at the titanium-clad miracle.” (lines 64 and 65) D: “However, he admits feeling pleased when people on the streets of Bilbao come up and give him a hug.” (lines 79 and 80)

nysed-ela-2016-g8-mc-q42.alt: Question 42. Which information would be most important to include in a summary of the article? Choices: A: “Shipyards sat idle; steel mills, silent.” (lines 3 and 4) B: “Their master plan included a first-class museum, so original that it would attract worldwide attention and rescue their town from its slow decline.” (lines 6 through 8) C: “You find his fish motif appearing here and there throughout the museum.” (lines 55 and 56) D: “At seventy Gehry finds his creativity is speeding up.” (line 81)
